
### binary translation  {.smaller}


* compile assembly to new assembly 
<hr class="vspace" />
* works without instruction set support
* early versions of VMWare on x86 (before x86 added virtualisation support)
* can be used to run one platform on another


### binary translation idea 

::: {.r-stack .my-full}

![](/vm/texfig/binTransIdea.figure-1.svg){.fragment .fade-in-then-out fragment-index=1}

![](/vm/texfig/binTransIdea.figure-2.svg){.fragment .fade-in-then-out fragment-index=2}

![](/vm/texfig/binTransIdea.figure-3.svg){.fragment .fade-in-then-out fragment-index=3}

:::


### a binary translation idea 


* convert whole <i>basic blocks</i> 

   * code upto branch/jump/call

* end with call to <code>translate_and_run</code>  

   * compute new <em>simulated PC</em> address to pass to call



### making binary translation fast  {.smaller}

<!-- \lstset{style=small,language=myasm,morekeywords={movq}} -->
 

* cache converted code 

   * <code>translate_and_run</code> checks cache first

* patch calls to <code>translate_and_run</code> to refer directly to cached code
* do something more clever than `movq rax_location, ...` 

   * map (some) registers to registers, not memory

* ends up being ‘‘just-in-time’’ compiler


### binary translation? really?  {.smaller}


* early VMWare: for instructions without hardware virtualization support 

   * only needed for little bits of OS code

* used by Apple to handle changing CPU designs
* Rosetta 2: run Intel on ARM (current)
* Rosetta: run Power PC on Intel (2005--2011)
* Mac 68k emulator: Run Motorola 680x0 on Power PC (1994--2005)
